items.headline: Kyverna Therapeutics plans to submit cell therapy for stiff person syndrome for FDA approval
items.preview: Kyverna Therapeutics has developed a personalized cell therapy that shows promise for treating stiff person syndrome. The company plans to submit this treatment for FDA approval by mid-year.
items.article:
A one-time, personalized cell therapy from Kyverna Therapeutics improved mobility and reduced disabilities in patients with stiff person syndrome, a rare, neurological autoimmune disorder, according to study results presented Tuesday.
Kyverna intends to submit the treatment to the Food and Drug Administration by the middle of the year. If approved, it would become the first treatment for stiff person syndrome and the first personalized CAR-T therapy for an autoimmune disease of any kind to reach the market.
Why this matters: Currently, CAR-T treatments are approved only for blood cancers, but using engineered T cells to deplete B cells has pushed a growing number of biotech companies to shift their CAR-T focus to autoimmune diseases.

sources.name: STAT Biotech news: Market-moving coverage of drug development

items.headline: New atopic dermatitis treatments in the pipeline for 2026
items.preview: A diverse pipeline of new treatments for atopic dermatitis is under investigation, focusing on targeted therapies. This includes several promising candidates that could challenge existing therapies.
items.article:
Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder characterized by eczematous lesions and intense itchiness, which can substantially impair an individual’s quality of life. Over the past decade, advances in immunological understanding have driven a therapeutic shift from broad immunosuppression toward more targeted, mechanism-based interventions.
This highlights the need for additional options, and, consequently, a diverse pipeline of new atopic dermatitis treatments is currently under investigation, spanning both validated pathways and novel immunological targets. Notable candidates include Connect Biopharma’s rademikibart and Apogee Therapeutics’ zumilokibart, both showing promising results in clinical trials.
Why this matters: The ongoing development of these therapies could significantly improve treatment options for patients who remain inadequately treated by existing therapies.

items.headline: Loss of DNA methyltransferase 3a enhances cardiomyocyte pyroptosis
items.preview: A study reveals that the loss of DNMT3a exacerbates myocardial injury by promoting pyroptosis in cardiomyocytes. This finding opens new avenues for therapeutic intervention in heart attack pathology.
items.article:
In a groundbreaking study published in Nature Communications, researchers have uncovered a pivotal molecular mechanism linking epigenetic regulation with cardiomyocyte cell death. The study reveals that the loss of DNA methyltransferase 3a (DNMT3a) exacerbates myocardial injury by enhancing the transcription of Caspase-8, a key mediator of inflammatory cell death known as pyroptosis.
This discovery highlights novel therapeutic possibilities to mitigate cardiac damage. The findings demonstrate that DNMT3a’s methylation activity normally represses Caspase-8 gene expression, preventing an exaggerated inflammatory response within the heart.
Why this matters: Targeting DNMT3a activity or blocking Caspase-8 offers new avenues to curb pyroptotic cardiomyocyte death, thereby limiting heart tissue damage and preserving cardiac function post-infarction.

items.headline: UCB acquires Neurona to enhance epilepsy portfolio
items.preview: UCB has acquired Neurona Therapeutics for $650 million, adding a regenerative cell therapy for epilepsy to its portfolio. This move strengthens UCB's commitment to developing treatments for epilepsy.
items.article:
A deal by UCB late Friday to acquire venture-backed Neurona will add another anticonvulsive treatment to the Belgian pharma’s portfolio. UCB S.A. announced it would pay $650 million to purchase Neurona Therapeutics Inc., whose lead asset, NRTX-1001, is a regenerative cell therapy comprising inhibitory GABAergic interneurons that secrete GABA.
This therapy is currently in a Phase I/II trial to treat mesial temporal lobe epilepsy, with plans for a Phase III study to start this year.
Why this matters: This acquisition enhances UCB's existing epilepsy portfolio and underscores the company's focus on innovative treatments for neurological disorders.

items.headline: The selfish ribosome: A new perspective on life's origin
items.preview: A new paper proposes that the ribosome evolved as a selfish symbiont, driving cellular processes and evolution. This perspective could reshape our understanding of the origin of life.
items.article:
This paper proposes that the ribosome originated as a mutualistic symbiont of an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase ribozyme, supplying peptides that enhanced replication. As life transitioned from the RNA to the RNA–protein world, autonomous replicators became irreversibly dependent on the ribosome for producing replication proteins.
The authors argue that the ribosome's evolution reflects a 'selfish' nature, where it consumes most of the cell’s resources while ensuring its propagation. This perspective suggests that the ribosome is not merely a workhorse of the cell but a primary driver of life’s evolution.
Why this matters: Understanding the ribosome's role in cellular processes could provide insights into the origin of life and the evolution of complex biological systems.
